perturbation.original_text: 2. Definition. “Confidential Information ” means any information, technical data, or know -how (including, but not limited to, information relating to research, products, software, services, development, inv entions, processes, engineering, marketing, techniques, customers, pricing, internal procedures, business and marketing plans or strategies, finances, employees and busi ness opportunities) disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Recipient, either directly or indirectly, in any form whatsoever (including, but not limited to, in writing, in machine readable or other tangible form, orally or visually): (i) that is a trade secret u nder applicable law; (ii) that has been marked as “confidential” or “proprietary ” or similar legend; (iii) whose confidential nature has been made known by the Disclosing Party, orally or in writing, to the Recipient; (iv) that due to its character and nature, a reasonable person under like circumstances would treat as confidential; o r (v) discussions relating to such information whether these discussions occur prior to, concurrent with, or following disclosure of such information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Definition. “Confidential Information ” means information disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the detailed definition of 'Confidential Information', subsequent sections referencing 'Confidential Information' become vague. Specifically, Section 5 refers to restrictions on disclosing 'Confidential Information', but without a clear definition, it's unclear what information falls under these restrictions. This creates an in-text contradiction, because it uses a term with a now vague definition.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Non -Disclosure. The Recipient agrees not to disclose the Confidential Information to any third parties or to any of its employees, dire ctors, officers, agents, contractors, consultants, advisors, or other representatives (collectively “ Representatives ”), except those Representatives who have a need to know the Confidential Information for accomplishing the Authorized Purpose described her ein. In such instance(s), the Representatives (i) shall be made aware that the Confidential Information is confidential; and (ii) that such Representatives’ disclosure of the Confidential Information is restricted as set forth herein. Notwithsta nding the foregoing, the Recipient may disclose the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information to the extent required by a valid order of a court of competent jurisdiction or other governmental body, or by applicable law; provided, however, that the Recipient will use all reasonable efforts to promptly notify the Disclosing Party of the obligation to make such disclosure in advance of the disclosure, unless otherwise prohibited by law or court order, so that the Disclosing Party will have a reasonable opportunity to object to such disclosure. The Recipient and its Representatives shall cooperate in all reasonable respects with the Disclosing Party in seeking to prevent or limit disclosure; and, in the event that a protective order or other remedy is not obtained, t he Recipient will limit disclosure to the Confidential Information actually required to be disclosed, provided the Recipient exercises its reasonable efforts to obtain reasonable assurances that confidential treatment will be accorded to the Disclosing Par ty’s Confidential Information. The Recipient agrees that it shall treat the Confidential Information with the same degree of care as it accords to its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature; provided that in no event shall the R ecipient exercise less than reasonable care to protect the Confidential Information commensurate with the sensitivity of such information. The Recipient agrees to advise the SUPPLIER MUTUAL CONFIDENTIAL NON- DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT SNDA-1 Rev D Page 2 of 3 Disclosing Party in writing of any misappropriation or misuse by any person of such Confidential Information of which the Recipient may become aware. The Recipient acknowledges and agrees that certain Confidential Information will be subject to export restrictions under the Arms Export Control Act (22 U.S.C. 2751 et seq.) or Executive Order 12470. In such instances, in addition to general non -disclosure restrictions set forth above, such Confidential Information shall not be transferred to any foreign person in the United States or abroad, except where authorized by the United States Department of State (ITAR) or the United States Department of Commerce (EAR). Such Confidential Information shall be appropriately marked and identified at the time of transfer.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the detailed definition of 'Confidential Information'. Section 5, the contradicted text, relies on a clear definition of 'Confidential Information' to establish restrictions on its disclosure. Without the original definition, the scope of what constitutes 'Confidential Information' becomes ambiguous, creating a direct conflict and uncertainty regarding which information is subject to the non-disclosure obligations. This aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction', where removing essential language creates a contradiction with obligations stated elsewhere.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Use Limitations. The Recipient (i) agrees not to use the Confidential Information for its own use or for any purposes except the Authorized Purpose expressly set forth above; (ii) shall not use the Confidential Information for purposes of unfair or improper competition; and (iii) agrees not to copy, alter, modify, disassemble, reverse engineer or decompile any of the materials unless permitted in writing by the Disclosing Party (any copies that are authorized to be made will be identified as belonging to th e Disclosing Party and marked as “confidential”, “proprietary” or with similar legend). Nothing in this Agreement shall limit or restrict the rights of the Disclosing Party to assert infringement or other intellectual property claims against the Recipien t or to impose on either party any obligation to disclose any Confident ial Information, to purchase or sell any products, or to otherwise enter in to any type of business relationship.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Use Limitations. The Recipient agrees not to use Confidential Information for its own use. Nothing in this Agreement shall limit or restrict the rights of the Disclosing Party to assert infringement or other intellectual property claims against the Recipien t or to impose on either party any obligation to disclose any Confident ial Information, to purchase or sell any products, or to otherwise enter in to any type of business relationship.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Authorized Purpose expressly set forth above', the definition of permitted use becomes unclear. This creates an internal contradiction because Section 1 mentions the 'Authorized Purpose', but Section 4 no longer defines what that purpose is in terms of usage limitation, which makes the 'Authorized Purpose' mentioned earlier, a contradictory statement, or confusing at the least.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Purpose. Each of the Company and Supplier desires to receive Confidential Information (as defined below) of the other for the purpose of con sidering a commercial transaction and/or ongoing business relationship with the other (the “ Authorized Purpose ”). For purposes of this Agreement, the party disclosing Confidential Information is hereinafter referred to as the “ Disclosing Party ” and the pa rty receiving Confidential Information is hereinafter referred to as the “ Recipient ”. In connection with the Authorized Purpose, certain trade secrets and business information proprietary to the Disclosing Party, and which the Disclosing Party considers confidential, may be provided to the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 4 defined the limitations of use, referring to the 'Authorized Purpose expressly set forth above.' The changed text removes this phrase, making the definition of permitted use unclear. Section 1 defines the 'Authorized Purpose' but Section 4, after the change, no longer provides usage limitations that connect to that purpose. This creates a contradiction because Section 1 references 'Authorized Purpose' which is no longer clearly limited in Section 4, based on the definition of 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Return of Materials. Any materials or documents of the Disclosing Party that are furnished to the Recipient, and all copies thereof, at the earlier of the Disclosing Party’s request for return of the materials, or the termination of the business relationship between the Disclosing Party and the Recipient, at the Disclosing Party’s option, will either be (subject to the Disclosing P arty’s reasonable instructions): (i) promptl y returned to the Disclosing Party and/or (ii) destroyed by the Recipient, including materials stored on the Recipient’s computing environment, or electronic storage media, to the extent technically feasible (with the Recipient providing written certification of such destruction).
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Return of Materials. Any materials or documents of the Disclosing Party that are furnished to the Recipient, and all copies thereof, at the earlier of the Disclosing Party’s request for return of the materials, or the termination of the business relationship between the Disclosing Party and the Recipient, will be returned to the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'at the Disclosing Party’s option, will either be (subject to the Disclosing P arty’s reasonable instructions): (i) promptl y returned to the Disclosing Party and/or (ii) destroyed by the Recipient, including materials stored on the Recipient’s computing environment, or electronic storage media, to the extent technically feasible (with the Recipient providing written certification of such destruction).' is omitted, this creates an in-text contradiction as it restricts the recipients option of what to do, instead of having the option of returning it to the disclosing party, or destroying it. It makes the recipient obliged to only send it back.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Return of Materials. Any materials or documents of the Disclosing Party that are furnished to the Recipient, and all copies thereof, at the earlier of the Disclosing Party’s request for return of the materials, or the termination of the business relationship between the Disclosing Party and the Recipient, will be returned to the disclosing party.
perturbation.justification: The original text provides the recipient with two options regarding the materials of the disclosing party: either return them or destroy them. The changed text removes the option of destruction, mandating that the materials must be returned. This creates a direct conflict with the original intent, as reflected in the contradicted text, which enforces only the return of the materials. Therefore, the change introduces an in-text contradiction as defined by the Omissions - In Text Contradiction type.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Remedies. The Recipient understands and agrees that the Disclosing Party is providing the Confidential Informat ion to the Recipient in reliance upon this Agreement, and the Recipient will be fully responsible to the Disclosing Party for any damage s or harm caused to the Disclosing Party by a breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives. T he Recipient acknowledges and agrees that a breach of any of its promises or agreements contained herein will result in irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party for which there will be no adequate remedy at law, and the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including specific performance and injunctive relief, in the event of any breach or threatened breach or intended breach of t his Agreement by the Recipient. Such remedies, however, shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for any breach of the Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Remedies. The Recipient understands and agrees that the Disclosing Party is providing the Confidential Informat ion to the Recipient in reliance upon this Agreement, and the Recipient will be fully responsible to the Disclosing Party for any damage s or harm caused to the Disclosing Party by a breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives. Such remedies, however, shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for any breach of the Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'The Recipient acknowledges and agrees that a breach of any of its promises or agreements contained herein will result in irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party for which there will be no adequate remedy at law, and the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including specific performance and injunctive relief, in the event of any breach or threatened breach or intended breach of t his Agreement by the Recipient.', the clause contradicts itself by stating remedies are non exclusive, but removing a key legal remedy. This makes the sentence 'Such remedies, however, shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for any breach of the Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.' contradictory as it suggests that there are more remedies that aren't there.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. Remedies. The Recipient understands and agrees that the Disclosing Party is providing the Confidential Informat ion to the Recipient in reliance upon this Agreement, and the Recipient will be fully responsible to the Disclosing Party for any damage s or harm caused to the Disclosing Party by a breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives. Such remedies, however, shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for any breach of the Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that a breach would result in irreparable injury and entitled the Disclosing Party to equitable relief. The changed text removes this specific entitlement to equitable relief (specific performance and injunctive relief), while still stating that the remedies are not exclusive. This creates a contradiction because the remaining text implies that other legal or equitable remedies are available, when a previously explicitly mentioned and crucial remedy has been removed. Since it removes a significant remedy but implies remedies are non-exclusive, it creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 12. Term; Termination. This Agreement shall govern all communications among the parties that are made from the date Confidential Inf ormation was first provided to or obtained by the Recipient (even if prior to the Effective Date) through the date on which a party receives from the other party written notice that such party is terminating this Agreement. The Recipient’s obligations und er this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information it has received or obtained prior to such termination shall continue until as long as the Confidential Information remains a trade secret or for a period of five (5) years after the dat e of disclos ure, whichever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. Term; Termination. This Agreement shall govern all communications among the parties that are made from the date Confidential Inf ormation was first provided to or obtained by the Recipient (even if prior to the Effective Date) through the date on which a party receives from the other party written notice that such party is terminating this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'The Recipient’s obligations und er this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information it has received or obtained prior to such termination shall continue until as long as the Confidential Information remains a trade secret or for a period of five (5) years after the dat e of disclos ure, whichever is later.', the text no longer dictates how long the agreement will last, this creates an in-text contradiction, as it states that the agreement terminates, but doesnt have a time definition of how long after termination the agreement will continue to be in force.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12. Term; Termination. This Agreement shall govern all communications among the parties that are made from the date Confidential Inf ormation was first provided to or obtained by the Recipient (even if prior to the Effective Date) through the date on which a party receives from the other party written notice that such party is terminating this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies the duration of obligations post-termination, either until the information ceases to be a trade secret or for five years after disclosure. The changed text omits this clause, leading to an in-text contradiction. The contradicted text is the same, but the change in the original text, by removing the section about the duration of the agreement after termination, directly contradicts itself and creates ambiguity about when the agreement truly ends. The omission creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Notices. All notices or other communications required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be in writing and addressed to the receiving party at its corporate office, or as otherwise designated in writing, an d shall be deemed effectively given on the earliest of : (i) when delivered, if personally delivered; (ii) on the third (3 rd) business day following the date of mailing if delivered by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested; (iii) on the d ate of transmission, if delivered by facsimile or email transmission; (iv) the scheduled day of delivery if delivered via express courier; or (v) when received by the party to whom notice is intended or required to be given.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Notices. All notices or other communications required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be in writing and addressed to the receiving party at its corporate office, or as otherwise designated in writing.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the delivery methods of the notices, this creates an in-text contradiction. The original text, mentions 'All notices or other communications required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be in writing and addressed to the receiving party at its corporate office, or as otherwise designated in writing'. But the text ends there, meaning that there is no definition on how the other party will be notified that the notices or other communications have been received.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 13. Notices. All notices or other communications required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be in writing and addressed to the receiving party at its corporate office, or as otherwise designated in writing.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines methods by which 'notices or other communications' can be delivered to the receiving party. The changed text removes these methods, creating a contradiction because the agreement states that notices 'shall be in writing and addressed to the receiving party', but provides no mechanism for ensuring the notice is effectively delivered or received. The contradicted text, which is the same as the changed text in this case, reinforces the contradiction, because it restates the notice requirement without providing the delivery mechanism. According to the Perturbation Definitions, this falls under the Omissions - In Text Contradiction.
